long_stay: Higher education programmes (Level 6 or above on the National Framework of Qualifications)
English language courses (minimum 25 weeks, 15 tuition hours/week)
qualifying_courses.notes: Courses must be full-time and in-person, listed on the Interim List of Eligible Programmes (ILEP) or TrustEd Ireland Providers list. Part-time, online, or distance learning courses do not qualify.

types.name: Short-stay Study Visa
types.notes: For stays of less than 3 months.

types.name: Long-stay Study Visa
types.notes: For stays of 3 months or more.

booking_methods.description: For document submission and biometrics at a Visa Application Centre (VAC).
For document submission and biometrics in countries without a VFS Global centre.

steps.title: Get Accepted by an Eligible Institution
steps.description: Apply and get accepted by an eligible Irish educational institution listed on the ILEP or TrustEd Ireland Providers list.

steps.title: Pay Tuition Fees
steps.description: Pay your tuition fees (either in full or a minimum of €6,000 upfront).

steps.title: Complete Online Application
steps.description: Complete the AVATS online application form, pay the visa fee, and print your summary sheet.

steps.title: Book and Attend Appointment
steps.description: Book an appointment via VFS Global or the Embassy to submit your physical documents and biometrics.

required_documents.name: Valid Passport
required_documents.details: Valid for at least 6 months past your intended course completion date.

required_documents.name: Printed and Signed AVATS Application Summary Form
Official Letter of Acceptance
required_documents.details: From an ILEP-listed Irish educational institution.

required_documents.name: Proof of Tuition Fees Paid
required_documents.details: Official receipt showing a minimum of €6,000 paid, or 100% of the fee if total tuition is under €6,000.

required_documents.name: Proof of English Language Proficiency
required_documents.details: e.g., IELTS, TOEFL, PTE, or a college statement on Irish-language ability if applicable.

required_documents.name: Statement of Purpose (SOP)
required_documents.details: Detailing why you want to study in Ireland and explaining any academic history gaps.

acceptable_proof: 6 months of consecutive bank statements on official bank paper

residence_permit.booking_method: Online via ISD Customer Service Portal (after arrival)
residence_permit.notes: First-time registrations for those living in Dublin are done at Burgh Quay. Those living outside Dublin register at their local immigration registration office (Garda station). A €300 registration fee is paid at the appointment. The IRP card (Stamp 2) is sent by post. Identity verification via a partner app (e.g., ID-Pal) may be required.

restrictions: Not permitted to engage in any business or trade (cannot own a company or operate as a sole trader)
Students with Stamp 2A permission are not allowed to work
Expanded work rights during holidays generally apply to students on programmes at NFQ Level 7 or above; English-language-only students have more limited work rights.

permit_renewal.notes: Renewals are processed online. Applicants must be present in Ireland. Stamp 2 applicants must wait until their course starts before applying to renew. A €300 annual renewal fee applies.

requirements: Graduation with an award at Level 8 (12 months permission) or Level 9+ (24 months permission) on the National Framework of Qualifications
Apply after receiving official final results and within six months of results, before Stamp 2 permission expires
Cannot operate a business or be self-employed under Stamp 1G status
Requires current IRP card, original letter from college confirming course completion and final results, valid medical insurance, and an application fee (around €300)
post_study_work.notes: After Stamp 1G, an employment permit from the Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment is required for long-term work.

official_sources.name: Immigration Service Delivery (ISD)
official_sources.description: Official Irish government immigration site covering visas, registration, renewals, and the Third Level Graduate Scheme.

official_sources.name: Citizens Information Ireland
official_sources.description: Official and reliable resource for general citizen-facing explainers.

common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Apply as close to the 3-month window as possible, especially during peak season (June–August).
Ensure all documents and passport details (name spelling, dates) match exactly.
Do not rely on money tied up in fixed deposits or investments; funds must be immediately accessible.
Always disclose any previous visa refusals truthfully.

common_mistakes.mistake: Missing the registration deadline after arrival
common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Book your IRP appointment promptly after landing, even though slots are only available post-arrival.
Ensure private health insurance is secured as it is compulsory for non-EU/EEA students and checked at multiple stages.

common_mistakes.mistake: Not enrolling on an ILEP-listed course
common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Ensure your chosen course is on the official Interim List of Eligible Programmes (ILEP) or TrustEd Ireland Providers list.
Keep funds stable for 6 months, or provide written proof of where the money came from (e.g., selling property, an official education loan sanction).

common_mistakes.consequence: Immigration officials may question genuine student intent
common_mistakes.how_to_avoid: Explain any time spent out of school or work clearly in your Statement of Purpose.
Ensure your university receipt clearly shows the required minimum €6,000 payment (or full amount if less than €6,000) and matches your paperwork.
